---
title: "Modelling"
---

In this part, I move from standalone inferential procedures to explicit statistical
models. The conceptual step is important: rather than asking only whether groups
differ or variables are associated, I begin to describe how a response varies with
predictors, what functional form that relationship takes, and whether the fitted
model is an adequate representation of the data.

This is where residuals, regression equations, model specification, interaction
terms, confounding, and model evaluation become central. The methods are more
flexible than the earlier tests, but they also demand more discipline in how the
model is formulated and checked.

Read this part as a continuation of inference rather than a departure from it.
The same statistical reasoning remains in place; the model simply becomes the
main unit of thought.
